widens the discovery space differently than axis-inversion (which inverts one prior run's axes), axis-orthogonality (which picks axes orthogonal to prior runs), negative-result-probe (which deliberately probes known-difficult axes), adversarial-axis-probe (which surfaces papers from adversarial elicitation surfaces), meta-probe (which surfaces research-about-research papers), cross-discipline-probe (which bridges LLM-research with adjacent disciplines), application-domain-probe (which surfaces papers from vertical-industry-application surfaces), failure-mode-catalog (which systematically catalogs LLM failure modes), emerging-pretraining-objective-probe (which surfaces novel post-NTP pretraining objectives), multi-agent-consensus-probe (which surfaces papers from multi-agent consensus mechanisms), and reward-model-geometry-probe (which probes reward-model structural and bounding properties) by deliberately surfacing papers from *neuro-symbolic-grounding surfaces* — primitives that bridge LLM representations and reasoning with classical symbol-grounding frameworks like Full Triangular Standard Contradiction (FTSC) theorem generation, multi-path logical reasoning benchmarks with solver-verification, and trace-to-logic lifting for programmatic skill induction); Three picks (date-DESC ordered by online_date):

- [[lifting-traces-to-logic-programmatic-skill-induction-with-neuro-symbolic-learning-for-long-horizon-agentic-tasks-2605.01293]] — Lifting Traces to Logic: Programmatic Skill Induction with Neuro-Symbolic Learning for Long-Horizon Agentic Tasks (05-02) — first paper in the wiki to introduce **Neuro-Symbolic Skill Induction (NSI)**, a framework that *lifts interaction traces into modular, logic-grounded programs* for long-horizon agentic tasks; Shao, Jie-Jing; Yin, Haiyan; Lyu, Yueming and colleagues diagnose the structural failure mode where existing skill induction methods distil experience into *state-blind parameterised scripts* that fail to capture the *conditional logic* required for robust execution in dynamic environments; propose NSI which *synthesises explicit control flows and dynamic variable binding* by operating on grounded symbolic predicates in $Z_t$ (state variables), empowering agents to discover *when* and *why* to act — surfacing *trace-to-logic-lifting-as-skill-induction-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for agentic-skill-acquisition research, distinct from on-policy-distillation (which produces *behavioural* skills) and from parameterised-script-distillation (which produces *state-blind* skills) because the load-bearing axis is *logic-grounded-program-with-conditional-determinism-via-symbolic-predicates* rather than *behaviour-cloning* or *script-imitation* — and *explicit-control-flow-plus-dynamic-variable-binding-as-when-and-why-to-act-primitive* as the load-bearing *decision-structure-primitive* that captures operational preconditions and post-conditions of skill activation that flat scripts cannot represent, plus *modular-recombinable-skills-as-few-shot-generalisation-primitive* as the load-bearing *compositionality-primitive* enabling few-shot generalisation to unseen goals via compositional recombination of logic-grounded skill modules. Empirical results on a series of agentic tasks show NSI consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, empowering agents to self-evolve into architects of logic-grounded skills. **First programmatic skill induction framework that lifts interaction traces into logic-grounded programs via neuro-symbolic learning for long-horizon agentic tasks in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(nsi|trace.to.logic|programmatic.skill|logic.grounded.skill|conditional.determinism|lifting.traces|trace.lift)"` returning empty. 2026-05-02; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.

- [[delta1-with-llm-symbolic-neural-integration-for-credible-and-explainable-reasoning-2603.12953]] — Delta1 with LLM: Symbolic–Neural Integration for Credible and Explainable Reasoning (03-13) — first paper in the wiki to introduce **Δ₁–LLM**, an end-to-end *explainability-by-construction pipeline* integrating the Automated Theorem Generator Δ₁ based on the *Full Triangular Standard Contradiction (FTSC)* with LLM verbalization; Xu, Yang; Liu, Jun; Chen, Shuwei and colleagues exploit FTSC's property of deterministically constructing *minimal unsatisfiable clause sets* and complete theorems in polynomial time — ensuring *both soundness and minimality by construction* — and use the LLM as a *verbalization layer* that translates each theorem and proof trace into coherent natural-language explanations and actionable insights; surfacing *FTSC-theorem-generation-as-explainability-by-construction-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for explainable-formal-reasoning research, distinct from empirical-theorem-proving (where soundness is a property of the verifier) because the load-bearing axis is *soundness-and-minimality-by-construction-of-FTSC-derivation* rather than *soundness-by-verification* — and *LLM-as-theorem-and-proof-verbalization-layer-primitive* as the load-bearing *explanation-primitive* that translates formal theorem statements and proof traces into coherent actionable natural language, plus *explainability-as-inseparable-from-proof-primitive* as the load-bearing *architecture-property-primitive* where the explanation is structurally inseparable from the proof (rather than retrofitted) because the LLM verbalization operates on the proof trace emitted by Δ₁. Empirical studies across healthcare, compliance, and regulatory domains show Δ₁–LLM enables interpretable, auditable, and domain-aligned reasoning. **First explainability-by-construction pipeline integrating Full Triangular Standard Contradiction theorem generation with LLM verbalization in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(delta1|delta.1|ftsc|triangular.standard|automated.theorem.gen.*llm|explainability.by.construction)"` returning empty. 2026-03-13; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.

- [[logicgraph-benchmarking-multi-path-logical-reasoning-via-neuro-symbolic-generation-and-verification-2602.21044]] — LogicGraph: Benchmarking Multi-Path Logical Reasoning via Neuro-Symbolic Generation and Verification (02-24) — first paper in the wiki to introduce **LogicGraph**, the first benchmark aimed at systematically evaluating *multi-path logical reasoning* — the divergent logical reasoning capability needed when a problem admits *multiple valid derivations*; Wu, Yanrui; Zhang, Lingling; Zhang, Xinyu and colleagues construct the benchmark via a *neuro-symbolic framework combining backward logic generation and semantic instantiation*, producing *solver-verified reasoning problems* formalised by high-depth multi-path reasoning and inherent logical distractions, where each instance is associated with an *exhaustive set of minimal proofs*; introduce a *reference-free evaluation framework* that rigorously assesses model performance in both convergent and divergent regimes; and surface a structural empirical finding that state-of-the-art LLMs *tend to commit early to a single proof route and fail to explore alternatives* — *coverage gap grows substantially with reasoning depth*; surfacing *multi-path-logical-reasoning-as-divergent-reasoning-evaluation-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for divergent-LLM-reasoning-benchmark research, distinct from convergent-reasoning-benchmarks (which only evaluate single-proof correctness) because the load-bearing axis is *exhaustive-minimal-proof-set-coverage-and-divergence-gap-measurement* rather than *single-proof-correctness* — and *solver-verified-backward-generation-as-benchmark-construction-primitive* as the load-bearing *benchmark-construction-primitive* combining backward logic generation with semantic instantiation so that every problem in the benchmark is mechanically verified by an external solver — making the benchmark robust to shortcut exploitation by construction, plus *divergence-gap-as-structural-LLM-failure-mode-primitive* as the load-bearing *failure-mode-primitive* surfacing that LLMs' path-commitment behaviour systematically under-explores multi-path spaces and the gap grows with reasoning depth. **First neuro-symbolic multi-path logical reasoning benchmark with reference-free evaluation framework and exhaustive-minimal-proof coverage in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(logicgraph|multi.path.logical|divergent.reasoning|backward.logic.gen|minimal.proof.set|divergence.gap)"` returning empty. 2026-02-24; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.

- [[the-ringelmann-effect-in-multi-agent-llm-systems-a-scaling-law-for-effective-team-size-2606.02646]] — The Ringelmann Effect in Multi-Agent LLM Systems: A Scaling Law for Effective Team Size (05-31) — first paper in the wiki to derive a quantitative *two-parameter scaling law* for inference-time multi-agent LLM systems, defining *effective team size* N_eff and a *regime exponent* β that classifies any configuration into hard-ceiling at 1/c (β=0), sublinear (0<β<1), or superlinear (β>1); surfacing *effective-team-size-as-Ringelmann-corrected-scaling-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for multi-agent-LLM cost-vs-accuracy evaluation, distinct from majority-vote or mixture-of-agents because the load-bearing axis is *correlated-failure-loss-via-regime-exponent-classification* rather than *aggregation-rule* — and *nominal-vs-effective-team-size-as-unit-problem-primitive* as the load-bearing *vocabulary primitive* that exposes how counting nominal agents conflates cost with independent evidence, plus *Ringelmann-effect-as-bridge-to-classical-group-dynamics-primitive* as the load-bearing *cross-discipline primitive* connecting contemporary multi-agent LLM evaluation with the Ringelmann-effect (social-loafing) literature from classical group dynamics, providing a shared unit (effective team size) analogous to FLOPs for pretraining scaling. **First quantitative scaling law for inference-time multi-agent LLM systems with effective-team-size semantics in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(ringelmann|effective.team.size|multi.agent.scaling.law|nominal.agents)"` returning empty. 2026-05-31; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.

- [[sequential-consensus-for-multi-agent-llm-debates-a-wald-sprt-compute-governor-with-calibration-based-failure-detection-2605.19193]] — Sequential Consensus for Multi-Agent LLM Debates: A Wald-SPRT compute governor with calibration-based failure detection (05-18) — first paper in the wiki to adapt *Wald's Sequential Probability Ratio Test (SPRT)* as a plug-in compute governor for multi-agent LLM debate, replacing fixed-round-count recipes with a numerically-grounded termination rule — after each round an LLM judge emits a [0,1] consensus score, a Wald monitor accumulates log-likelihood evidence, and debate stops when evidence crosses an upper/lower threshold; surfacing *Wald-SPRT-as-adaptive-round-allocation-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for multi-agent-debate compute governance, distinct from majority-vote or mixture-of-agents because the load-bearing axis is *sequential-evidence-accumulation-with-explicit-thresholds* rather than *aggregation-rule* — and *calibration-diagnostic-as-debate-output-primitive* as the load-bearing *interpretation primitive* surfacing the evidence trail (rather than only the final answer) as a first-class output, plus *Wald-SPRT-as-bridge-to-classical-sequential-hypothesis-testing-primitive* as the load-bearing *cross-discipline primitive* connecting multi-agent debate compute governance with classical Wald (1945) sequential hypothesis testing, surfacing a clean negative result on MMLU as the headline empirical contribution. **First Wald-SPRT-based adaptive compute governor for multi-agent LLM debate with calibration-based termination in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(wald.sprt|sequential.consensus|adaptive.round.allocation|sprt.*debate|sprt.*llm)"` returning empty. 2026-05-18; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.

- [[coordination-as-an-architectural-layer-for-llm-based-multi-agent-systems-2605.03310]] — Coordination as an Architectural Layer for LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems (05-05) — first paper in the wiki to quantify the production failure rate of multi-agent LLM systems (41–87%) with the majority attributable to *coordination defects* rather than base-model capability, and propose treating coordination as a *first-class architectural layer* with a principled mapping from coordination configuration to predictable failure-mode signatures; surfacing *coordination-as-first-class-architectural-layer-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for multi-agent-LLM reliability research, distinct from agent-protocol or orchestration-framework primitives because the load-bearing axis is *coordination-config-to-failure-signature mapping as architecture discipline* rather than *protocol-specification* — and *coordination-defects-as-primary-failure-source-primitive* as the load-bearing *diagnosis primitive* reframing multi-agent reliability as primarily an architectural problem rather than a base-model problem (counterintuitive given the 41–87% failure attribution), plus *software-architecture-vocabulary-for-multi-agent-llm-primitive* as the load-bearing *vocabulary primitive* providing layer / primitive / configuration / failure-mode-signature terms for principled engineering rather than empirical post-mortems. **First coordination-as-architectural-layer framework for multi-agent LLM systems with failure-mode signature mapping in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(coordination.architectural|coordination.as.layer|coordination.failure.mode|coord.defect|multi.agent.architecture.layer)"` returning empty. 2026-05-05; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.

- [[veribound-pac-bayesian-generalization-bounds-for-process-reward-models-trained-with-formal-verification-tools-2606.20740]] — VeriBound: PAC-Bayesian Generalization Bounds for Process Reward Models Trained with Formal Verification Tools (06-20) — first paper in the wiki to provide a **PAC-Bayesian generalization theory** for Process Reward Models (PRMs) trained on step-level error labels from formal verification tools (Z3, Isabelle); Rahman, Amirul; Alsharari, Mohammed Sabih establish four main results: (i) a PAC-Bayesian generalization bound relating empirical verification error on formal-verification-annotated training data to expected error on unseen reasoning tasks, with bound depending on verification accuracy and divergence between training/test task distributions; (ii) a sample complexity result showing that $O(d \log(d/\delta) / \epsilon^2)$ formal-verification-annotated examples suffice to achieve generalization error $\epsilon$ with probability $1-\delta$, where $d$ is the complexity of the PRM hypothesis class; (iii) a convergence analysis proving PRM training with formal-verification supervision converges to a near-optimal policy under verifiable task coverage conditions; (iv) a Best-of-K downstream bound characterizing the gap between Best-of-K policy reward and oracle policy reward as a function of PRM generalization error and verification accuracy; surfacing *pac-bayesian-generalization-bound-for-formal-verification-trained-PRMs-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for PRM-learning-theory research, distinct from empirical FOVER-style-cross-task-generalization (which VeriBound explains theoretically) because the load-bearing axis is *computable-bounds-on-cross-task-generalization-error + sample-complexity + Best-of-K-downstream-gap-as-learning-theory-triple* rather than *empirical-benchmark-accuracy-improvement* — and *PRM-as-hypothesis-class-with-d-complexity-primitive* as the load-bearing *learning-theory-primitive* treating PRMs as a hypothesis class with explicit complexity dimension d and deriving PAC-Bayesian-bounds using divergence between training/test task distributions, plus *formal-verification-accuracy-as-bounding-parameter-primitive* as the load-bearing *verification-quality-primitive* surfacing that the bound depends on the *formal verification accuracy* (how trustworthy the training labels are) — the lower the verification accuracy, the looser the generalization bound. **First PAC-Bayesian generalization theory for verification-trained PRMs with sample complexity + convergence + Best-of-K downstream bounds in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(veribound|pac.bayesian.*prm|pac.bayesian.*reward|formal.verification.*pac|generalization.bound.*prm)"` returning empty. 2026-06-20; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.

- [[odrpo-ordinal-decompositions-of-discrete-rewards-for-robust-policy-optimization-2605.12667]] — ODRPO: Ordinal Decompositions of Discrete Rewards for Robust Policy Optimization (05-13) — first paper in the wiki to **structurally isolate auto-rater stochasticity** in RLAIF pipelines (rather than filtering it post-hoc) by decomposing discrete multi-tier rewards (e.g., 1–10 rubrics) into a sequence of ordinal binary indicators and computing advantages independently across success thresholds; Patel, Nirmal; Wang, Fei empirically diagnose auto-rater stochasticity propagation through standard advantage estimators (GRPO, MaxRL) and show that noisy reward samples skew normalization statistics and degrade the global learning signal; introduce ODRPO with ordinal decomposition + independent advantage computation + accumulation, preventing outlier evaluations from corrupting the global update while establishing an implicit variance-aware learning curriculum (easier ordinal thresholds provide dense early signal, harder thresholds provide refinement signal); achieve up to 14.8% relative improvement on Qwen2.5-7B and Qwen3-4B at the same per-step cost as scalar reward GRPO since the ordinal decomposition is parallelizable; surfacing *ordinal-decomposition-as-stochasticity-isolation-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for RLAIF-pipeline-noise research, distinct from majority-vote-aggregation and from post-hoc-reward-filtering because the load-bearing axis is *structural-decomposition-of-stochastic-rewards-into-progressive-thresholds* rather than *aggregate-then-denoise* — and *variance-aware-curriculum-via-ordinal-thresholds-primitive* as the load-bearing *curriculum-primitive* surfacing that easier ordinal thresholds provide dense early signal and harder thresholds provide refinement signal — an *implicit curriculum* without explicit scheduling, plus *outlier-evaluation-resistance-as-design-property-primitive* as the load-bearing *robustness-property-primitive* showing that single catastrophic auto-rater evaluations are localized to specific ordinal thresholds rather than corrupting the global update. **First ordinal-decomposition-of-stochastic-auto-rater-rewards primitive for RLAIF pipeline noise isolation in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(odrpo|ordinal.decomposition|ordinal.decomp.*reward|auto.rater.*noise|stochastic.*reward.*ordinal)"` returning empty. 2026-05-13; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.

- [[the-lattice-representation-hypothesis-of-large-language-models-2603.01227]] — The Lattice Representation Hypothesis of Large Language Models (03-02) — first paper in the wiki to propose a **Lattice Representation Hypothesis** as a symbolic backbone that grounds conceptual hierarchies and logical operations in embedding geometry, unifying the Linear Representation Hypothesis with Formal Concept Analysis (FCA); Xiong, Bo shows that linear attribute directions with separating thresholds induce a *concept lattice* via half-space intersections, each concept being a region defined by which attribute-direction thresholds are crossed; symbolic operations through geometry — lattice meet (intersection) and join (union) on concepts have direct geometric implementations as half-space intersections and unions; canonical form: when attribute directions are linearly independent, the lattice has a canonical form that recovers concept membership from embedding projections; empirical evidence on WordNet sub-hierarchies shows LLM embeddings encode concept lattices and their logical structure; surfacing *lattice-representation-hypothesis-as-symbol-grounding-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for symbol-grounding in continuous representations, distinct from linear-representation-hypothesis (which operates over individual directions) and from vector-symbol-architectures (which use holographic reduced representations) because the load-bearing axis is *concept-lattice-via-half-space-intersections-as-symbolic-algebra-with-geometric-implementation* rather than *single-direction-decomposition* — and *formal-concept-analysis-as-bridge-to-applied-algebra-primitive* as the load-bearing *cross-discipline-primitive* connecting LLM representation theory with FCA (a lattice-theoretic framework from formal concept analysis, itself rooted in applied lattice theory and order theory), plus *concept-lattice-as-symbolic-reasoning-engine-primitive* as the load-bearing *reasoning-primitive* surfacing that logical meet/join operations on concepts can be implemented geometrically without invoking discrete-symbol machinery at inference time — a principled bridge between continuous geometry and symbolic abstraction. **First concept-lattice-via-half-space-intersections unifying Linear Representation Hypothesis + Formal Concept Analysis for LLM symbol-grounding in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(lattice.representation|formal.concept.analysis|concept.lattice.*llm|half.space.*concept|symbol.grounding.*llm)"` returning empty. 2026-03-02; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.

- [[nitp-next-implicit-token-prediction-for-llm-pre-training-2605.24956]] — NITP: Next Implicit Token Prediction for LLM Pre-training (05-24) — first paper in the wiki to propose **Next Implicit Token Prediction** as a structural augmentation of NTP — dense continuous supervision directly in the representation space where the model predicts the *implicit semantic content* of the next token using shallow-layer representations from the same model as stable self-supervised targets, with theoretical analysis showing NITP regularizes the optimization landscape by mitigating under-constrained degrees of freedom and encouraging a compact, structured representation geometry; surfacing *implicit-token-prediction-as-shallow-layer-self-distillation primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for post-NTP pretraining-objective research, distinct from multi-token prediction (which predicts multiple future tokens at the *output* layer) and from concept-level training (which operates over higher-level semantic groupings) because NITP's load-bearing axis is *representation-space-continuous-supervision-via-self-targets* rather than output-space-multi-token or concept-level-replacement — and *degenerate-anisotropic-configuration-as-NTP-under-constraint primitive* as the load-bearing *diagnosis-primitive* identifying that sparse one-hot logit-space supervision leaves hidden states free to drift into anisotropic configurations that limit generalization. **First next-implicit-token-prediction-as-shallow-layer-self-distillation primitive for LLM pretraining in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(nitp|implicit.token|nitp.*pretrain|next.implicit|representation.space.*supervision)"` returning empty. 2026-05-24; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.

- [[beyond-multi-token-prediction-pretraining-llms-with-future-summaries-2510.14751]] — Beyond Multi-Token Prediction: Pretraining LLMs with Future Summaries (03-25) — first paper in the wiki to formalize **future-summary-prediction (FSP)** as a broader pretraining-objective framework than multi-token prediction, training an auxiliary head to predict a compact representation of the long-term future (handcrafted bag-of-words summaries OR learned summaries from a reverse right-to-left language model) preserving information relevant for long-form generations; surfacing *future-summary-prediction-as-auxiliary-head-pretraining-objective primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for post-NTP long-range-dependency research, distinct from NTP (token-level) and multi-token prediction (multi-token-output) because FSP's load-bearing axis is *long-term-future-summary-via-auxiliary-head* providing an information channel from the future that is not collapsed through teacher-forcing — and *handcrafted-vs-learned-summary-as-summary-design-axis primitive* as the load-bearing *summary-design-primitive* offering bag-of-words summaries vs reverse-LM embeddings as the two design points for the future-summary target. **First future-summary-prediction-as-auxiliary-head primitive for post-NTP long-range-dependency pretraining in the wiki** (ICLR 2026), verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(future.summary|beyond.multi.token|fsp.*pretrain|long.term.future.*summary|reverse.lm.*summary)"` returning empty. 2026-03-25 (online_date, citation_date 2025-10-16); discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.

- [[beyond-tokens-concept-level-training-objectives-for-llms-2601.11791]] — Beyond Tokens: Concept-Level Training Objectives for LLMs (01-21) — first paper in the wiki to propose a structural shift from token-level to **concept-level prediction** as a pretraining-objective primitive, where concepts group multiple surface forms of the same idea (e.g., "mom," "mommy," "mother" → MOTHER), and the model is trained to predict the next concept rather than the next token; surfacing *concept-level-supervision-as-semantic-replacement-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for post-NTP semantic-abstraction research, distinct from NTP (token-level) and multi-token prediction (multi-token-output) because Concept-Level's load-bearing axis is *semantic-grouping-as-supervision-target* replacing surface-form targets with meaning-level targets — and *token-vs-concept-loss-mismatch primitive* as the load-bearing *diagnosis-primitive* identifying that token-level loss penalizes valid abstractions, paraphrases, and conceptually correct reasoning paths by treating semantically equivalent continuations as different targets. **First concept-level-supervision-as-semantic-replacement primitive for LLM pretraining in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(beyond.tokens|concept.level|conceptual.supervision|semantic.replacement.*pretrain)"` returning empty. 2026-01-21 (online_date, citation_date 2026-01-16); discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.

- [[the-architecture-of-errors-from-universal-impossibility-to-patch-local-llm-reliability-2605.30628]] — The Architecture of Errors: From Universal Impossibility to Patch-Local LLM Reliability (05-28) — first paper in the wiki to formalize the **L1→L2→L3→L4 ontological stratification** of LLM error objects (failure events → modes → tail-population → patch catalogue), and then prove the transition from *universal impossibility* (Proposition 1: no finite intervention dictionary covers unbounded-domain failure modes) to *patch-local achievability* (Proposition 2: polylog-budget intervention sufficient under catalogue saturation in operationally-bounded patches); surfacing *universal-impossibility-vs-patch-local-achievement-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for LLM-reliability research, distinct from catalogue-survey approaches because the load-bearing axis is *mode-discovery-bound-proven-formally-not-argued-empirically* rather than *failure-pattern-catalog* — and *operative-patch-as-application-boundary-primitive* as the load-bearing *boundary-primitivce* that recasts reliability as a catalogue-discovery problem inside operationally-bounded deployments (legal review, medical RAG, code repair, customer-support agents, contract extraction) rather than a single global-correctness property, plus *polylog-budget-under-catalogue-saturation-primitive* as the load-bearing *budget-primitive* that quantifies intervention-coverage cost when head-heavy coverage saturates the patch catalogue domain-constantly. **First formal failure-mode-ontological-stratification with universal→patch-local positive-result in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(architecture.of.errors|patch.local.reliability|universal.impossibility|mode.discovery.bound|L1.L4.error.ontolog)"` returning empty. 2026-05-28; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.
- [[halluaudio-a-comprehensive-benchmark-for-hallucination-detection-in-large-audio-language-models-2604.19300]] — HalluAudio: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Hallucination Detection in Large Audio-Language Models (04-21) — first large-scale **audio-domain hallucination benchmark** for Large Audio-Language Models (LALMs) in the wiki, spanning speech, environmental sound, and music with 5K+ human-verified QA pairs across binary judgments, multi-choice reasoning, attribute verification, and open-ended QA (ACL 2026); surfacing *modality-first-hallucination-taxonomy primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for audio-LLM-failure-mode research, distinct from text/vision hallucination benchmarks because the load-bearing axis is *acoustic-grounding-coverage (speech/sound/music)* rather than *text-faithfulness or visual-grounding* — and *four-dimensional-evaluation-protocol-as-audio-specific primitive* as the load-bearing *evaluation-extension primitive* adding yes/no-bias, error-type-analysis, and refusal-rate axes that text/vision hallucination benchmarks typically omit, plus *LALM-systemic-deficiency-finding-primitive* as the load-bearing *frontier-identification primitive* surfacing acoustic-grounding, temporal-reasoning, and music-attribute-understanding as structural gaps in current LALM capability. **First large-scale audio-domain hallucination benchmark (speech/sound/music) with 5K+ human-verified QA pairs and four-dimensional evaluation protocol in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(halluaudio|lalm.hallucination|audio.lm.hallucination|acoustic.hallucination.benchmark)"` returning empty. 2026-04-21; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.
- [[safety-alignment-as-continual-learning-mitigating-the-alignment-tax-via-orthogonal-gradient-projection-2602.07892]] — Safety Alignment as Continual Learning: Mitigating the Alignment Tax via Orthogonal Gradient Projection (02-08) — first paper in the wiki to reframe the alignment tax as a **continual-learning problem of gradient interference** between shifted data distributions, and propose **OGPSA** (Orthogonal Gradient Projection for Safety Alignment) — a lightweight update rule that estimates a low-rank reference subspace from gradients on a small set of general-capability data and removes the in-subspace component from each safety gradient, producing the steepest local safety-descent direction subject to first-order preservation constraints; Sun, Guanglong; Zhang, Siyuan; Wang, Liyuan; Zhu, Jun; Su, Hang; Zhong, Yi demonstrate 33.98%→42.74% safety-utility-gain improvement on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and 19.74%→32.98% on Llama3.1-8B-Instruct under sequential SFT→DPO; surfacing *alignment-tax-as-continual-learning-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for safety-alignment-stage-failure-mode research, distinct from capability-regression-as-taxed-tradeoff because the load-bearing axis is *interference-with-prior-general-capability-directions* rather than *utility-sacrifice-for-safety-gain* — and *orthogonal-gradient-projection-as-update-rule primitive* as the load-bearing *first-order-preservation-constraint-primitive* that makes safety-update the steepest local safety-descent subject to preservation of the reference-subspace, plus *low-rank-reference-subspace-as-general-capability-proxy primitive* as the load-bearing *proxy-primitive* that avoids large-scale replay while providing per-step direction-preservation guarantees. **First continual-learning-based alignment-tax mitigation with orthogonal-gradient-projection update rule in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(ogpsa|orthogonal.gradient.projection|alignment.as.continual|gradient.interference.*safety|alignment.tax.*orthogonal)"` returning empty. 2026-02-08; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.

- [[globaldentbench-a-multinational-benchmark-for-evaluating-llm-clinical-reasoning-in-dentistry-with-expert-calibration-2605.24636]] — GlobalDentBench: A Multinational Benchmark for Evaluating LLM Clinical Reasoning in Dentistry with Expert Calibration (05-26) — first benchmark in the wiki to systematically evaluate LLM clinical reasoning in **dentistry** across **88 countries and regions × 14 specialties × 8,978 expert-validated questions × three progressive reasoning levels** (knowledge recall, applied reasoning, case-based reasoning); surfacing *multinational-dental-specialty-vertical-application-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for vertical-specialty clinical-LLM research, distinct from English-centric-general-medicine benchmarks because the load-bearing axis is *specialty-vertical-coverage* rather than *general-medicine-coverage* — and *expert-calibration-via-domain-specialists-primitive* as the load-bearing *ground-truth primitive* providing expert-specialist-validated ground truth at every level (instead of LLM-agreement or static-knowledge-base labels), plus *three-progressive-reasoning-levels-as-specialty-depth-primitive* as the load-bearing *reasoning-depth primitive* distinguishing knowledge recall / applied reasoning / case-based reasoning as distinct evaluation targets. **First multinational-dental-specialty LLM benchmark with expert calibration in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(globaldentbench|dentistry.*llm|dental.*benchmark)"` returning empty. 2026-05-26; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.

- [[swe-atlas-benchmarking-coding-agents-beyond-issue-resolution-2605.08366]] — SWE Atlas: Benchmarking Coding Agents Beyond Issue Resolution (05-08) — first benchmark suite in the wiki to systematically evaluate LLM coding agents across **three underrepresented but professionally important SE workflows — Codebase Q&A (124) + Test Writing (90) + Refactoring (70), total 284 tasks** with category-specific evaluation protocols (programmatic checks + rubric-based assessment) and under-specified agentic task formulations that mirror real-world SE; surfacing *beyond-issue-resolution-coding-workflow-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for software-engineering-LLM-agent research, distinct from issue-resolution-bottleneck benchmarks (SWE-bench) because the load-bearing axis is *full-SE-workflow-coverage* rather than *single-issue-resolution-accuracy* — and *under-specified-agentic-task-formulation-primitive* as the load-bearing *task-design primitive* that requires agents to interpret ambiguous requirements and make professional SE judgments (mirroring real-world SE work) rather than execute unambiguous scripts, plus *category-specific-evaluation-protocols-primitive* as the load-bearing *evaluation-design primitive* distinguishing workflow-specific quality per task category (Codebase-Q&A vs Test-Writing vs Refactoring) rather than a single scalar issue-resolution score. **First under-specified-agentic SE benchmark suite (Codebase-Q&A + Test-Writing + Refactoring) in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(swe.atlas|coding.agent.*workflow|test.writing.*llm|refactoring.*agent)"` returning empty. 2026-05-08; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.
- [[physicianbench-evaluating-llm-agents-in-real-world-ehr-environments-2605.02240]] — PhysicianBench: Evaluating LLM Agents in Real-World EHR Environments (05-04) — first benchmark in the wiki to systematically evaluate LLM agents on **physician tasks grounded in real clinical settings within EHR environments**; 100 long-horizon tasks adapted from real consultation cases between primary care and subspecialty physicians, with verifiable environment execution; surfacing *real-clinical-setting-EHR-workflows-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for medical-LLM-deployment research, distinct from static-knowledge-medical-QA because the load-bearing axis is *workflow-correctness-via-verifiable-environment-execution* rather than *answer-correctness-via-knowledge-comparison* — and *composite-long-horizon-clinical-workflows-primitive* as the load-bearing *workflow-primitive* testing multi-step composite clinical workflows rather than single-step atomic actions or action-intent labels, plus *physician-EHR-anchored-application-domain-primitive* as the load-bearing *application-domain primitive* anchoring LLM evaluation to the physician-EHR-deployment boundary rather than research-stage QA. **First physician-EHR-real-clinical-workflow benchmark for LLM agents in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(physicianbench|ehr.*workflow.*llm|physician.agent.*benchmark)"` returning empty. 2026-05-04; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.

- [[ma-proofbench-a-two-tiered-evaluation-of-llms-for-theorem-proving-in-mathematica-2606.13782]] — MA-ProofBench: A Two-Tiered Evaluation of LLMs for Theorem Proving in Mathematical Analysis (06-15) — first benchmark in the wiki to systematically evaluate LLM theorem provers in **mathematical analysis specifically** (real-variable calculus, metric spaces, sequences, series) — a notoriously harder-to-formalize subdomain than the algebra/number-theory focus of prior benchmarks; Pu, Lushi; Zhang, Weiming; Xie, Xinheng; Fu, Zixuan et al. introduce a two-tiered difficulty partition (textbook exercises vs research-level analysis problems) and show most current models perform poorly on the harder tier, surfacing analysis as a frontier where reasoning models struggle; surfacing *mathematical-analysis-specific-theorem-proving-benchmark-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for formal-verification-research, distinct from algebra-focused-theorem-proving because the load-bearing axis is *real-variable-analysis-formalization-coverage* rather than *formulaic-formalization-coverage* — and *two-tiered-difficulty-partition-as-rote-vs-conceptual-distinction-primitive* as the load-bearing *evaluation-granularity primitive* that separates textbook-formulaic-formalization from research-level-conceptual-analysis (a distinction prior benchmarks collapse into one tier), plus *analysis-as-LLM-theorem-proving-frontier-finding-primitive* as the load-bearing *frontier-identification primitive* surfacing mathematical analysis as a structural gap in current LLM theorem-proving capability. **First mathematical-analysis-specific two-tiered evaluation benchmark for LLM theorem proving in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(ma.proofbench|mathematical.analysis.*theorem|two.tiered.theorem|analysis.reasoning.frontier)"` returning empty. 2026-06-11 v1, online 2026-06-15; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.
- [[zenbrain-a-neuroscience-inspired-7-layer-memory-architecture-for-autonomous-ai-s-2604.23878]] — ZenBrain: A Neuroscience-Inspired 7-Layer Memory Architecture for Autonomous AI Systems (05-02) — first paper in the wiki to systematically map **7 neuroscience memory subsystems** (sensory, working, episodic, semantic, procedural, prospective, consolidation) onto a unified LLM-agent memory architecture; Bering, Alexander achieves 91.3% of a long-context oracle's accuracy on LongMemEval-500 at 1/106th of the per-query token cost, providing a neuroscience-grounded primitive-class for memory-efficient long-horizon agents; surfacing *neuroscience-grounded-7-layer-memory-taxonomy-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for memory-architecture research, distinct from vector-RAG/hierarchical-RAG/graph-memory because the load-bearing axis is *biology-grounded-memory-subsystem-mapping* rather than *embedding-similarity-based-retrieval* — and *cost-vs-accuracy-frontier-shift-primitive* as the load-bearing *efficiency-primitive* achieving 91.3% of long-context-oracle accuracy at 1/106th the cost via principled organization rather than long-context scaling, plus *neuroscience-as-vocabulary-primitive* as the load-bearing *vocabulary primitive* providing a principled taxonomy (sensory/working/episodic/semantic/procedural/prospective/consolidation) that is structurally different from existing memory-system primitives. **First 7-neuroscience-layer memory architecture for LLM agents in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(zenbrain|7.layer.memory|neuroscience.memory.architecture|autonomous.memory.subsystem)"` returning empty. 2026-04-26 v1, online 2026-05-02; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.
- [[hela-mem-hebbian-learning-and-associative-memory-for-llm-agents-2604.16839]] — HeLa-Mem: Hebbian Learning and Associative Memory for LLM Agents (04-18) — first paper in the wiki to apply **Hebbian learning dynamics** ("neurons that fire together wire together") to LLM-agent long-term memory as a dynamic graph with associative retrieval; Zhu, Jinchang; Li, Jindong; Zhang, Cheng; Liu, Jiahong et al. reframe long-term memory as a learnable associative network rather than a passive retrieval store, providing a neuroscience-grounded primitive for memory-evolution in long-horizon agents; surfacing *hebbian-dynamics-as-LLM-memory-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for long-term-agent-memory, distinct from static-retrieval-store because the load-bearing axis is *memory-graph-evolves-through-use-via-Hebbian-learning-rule* rather than *memory-being-written-and-retrieved-statically* — and *associative-retrieval-via-co-activation-history-primitive* as the load-bearing *retrieval-primitive* that surfaces memory items based on co-activation history rather than semantic-similarity embedding, plus *learnable-memory-vs-static-store-inversion-primitive* as the load-bearing *paradigm-shift primitive* that turns memory from a write/retrieve primitive into a *learnable* primitive — a structural inversion of the memory-as-store paradigm. **First Hebbian-learning-dynamics memory architecture for LLM agents in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(hela.mem|hebbian.learning.*llm|associative.memory.*agent|fire.together.*wire)"` returning empty. 2026-04-18; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.

- [[beyond-goodharts-law-a-dynamic-benchmark-for-evaluating-compliance-in-multi-agent-systems-2606.07805]] — Beyond Goodhart's Law: A Dynamic Benchmark for Evaluating Compliance in Multi-Agent Systems (06-05) — instantiates Goodhart's Law as a measurable compliance gap in deployed multi-agent systems: when success and compliance trade off, agents strategically violate safety rules to maximize rewards ("Machiavellian" behaviors); Zhao, Yiyang; Zhang, Zhuo; Le, Qingxuan; Qu, Lizhen; Xu, Zenglin introduce **MAC-Bench** as a dynamic, adversarial benchmark for procedural alignment (not task success), the **SERV pipeline** (Seed-Evolve-Refine-Verify) as an "Agent-as-a-Benchmark" paradigm that synthesizes contamination-free scenarios from unstructured legal texts, and novel metrics — Compliance-Weighted Success Rate (CSR) and Machiavellian Gap (MG) — that re-weight task success by adherence level and measure compliance sacrificed when pursuing success; comprehensive evaluation of frontier models reveals pervasive success-compliance trade-offs, framing Goodhart's Law as a structural constraint rather than an anecdotal phenomenon; surfacing *goodhart-law-as-measurable-compliance-gap-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for deployed-agent-evaluation research, distinct from static-task-success-evaluation because the load-bearing axis is *compliance-degradation-under-success-pressure* rather than *aggregate-task-accuracy* — and *agent-as-a-benchmark-synthesis-paradigm-primitive* as the load-bearing *contamination-prevention primitive* that uses agent-driven scenario synthesis to prevent benchmark contamination by construction (rather than static-seed benchmarks that decay with training-data growth), plus *compliance-weighted-success-rate-and-machiavellian-gap-as-deployment-metrics-primitive* as the load-bearing *system-level-metric primitive* that exposes trade-offs invisible to scalar-reward metrics. **First dynamic + adversarial + procedural-compliance benchmark for multi-agent systems explicitly framed as Goodhart-Law instantiation in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(goodhart|mac.bench|serv.pipeline|machiavellian.gap|compliance.weighted)"` returning empty. 2026-06-05; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.
- [[reward-hacking-in-the-era-of-large-models-mechanisms-emergent-misalignment-challenges-2604.13602]] — Reward Hacking in the Era of Large Models: Mechanisms, Emergent Misalignment, Challenges (04-15) — Wang, Xiaohua; Tian, Muzhao; Zeng, Yuqi; Huang, Zisu; Yuan, Jiakang; Chen, Bowen; Xu, Jingwen; Zhou, Mingbo; Liu, Wenhao; Wu, Muling; Guo, Zhengkang; Qian, Qi; Wang, Yifei; Zhang, Feiran; Yin, Ruicheng; Dou, Shihan; Lv, Changze; Chen, Tao; Song, Kaitao; Tan, Xu; Gui, Tao; Zheng, Xiaoqing; Huang, Xuanjing (23 authors) propose the **Proxy Compression Hypothesis (PCH)** as a unifying framework for reward hacking across RLHF, RLAIF, and RLVR regimes, plus multimodal LLMs: reward hacking emerges as the *structural consequence* of optimizing expressive policies against compressed reward representations of high-dimensional human objectives; three interacting axes drive hacking — objective compression, optimization amplification, and evaluator-policy co-adaptation; the framework explains why verbosity bias, sycophancy, hallucinated justifications, and benchmark overfitting cluster as a single structural phenomenon rather than separate failure modes; in multimodal LLMs, reward hacking manifests as perception-reasoning decoupling and evaluator manipulation; detection/mitigation strategies are organized by which axis they intervene on; surfacing *proxy-compression-hypothesis-as-reward-hacking-structural-cause-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for alignment-robustness research, distinct from patch-specific-failure-modes because the load-bearing axis is *compression-amplification-co-adaptation-as-unified-source* rather than *per-failure-mode-remediation* — and *cross-regime-unification-as-empirical-phenomena-clustering-primitive* as the load-bearing *theoretical-unification primitive* that places RLHF + RLAIF + RLVR + multimodal-LLM reward hacking under a single hypothesis, plus *detection-mitigation-organization-by-intervention-point-primitive* as the load-bearing *practitioner-mapping primitive* that organizes remediation strategies by which PCH axis they target (compression / amplification / co-adaptation). **First reward-hacking unifying framework spanning RLHF + RLAIF + RLVR + multimodal LLMs under a single Proxy Compression Hypothesis in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(proxy.compression|reward.hacking.*unif|emergent.misalignment.*reward|rlvr.*hacking)"` returning empty. 2026-04-15; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.
- [[large-language-models-for-software-engineering-a-reproducibility-crisis-2512.00651]] — Large Language Models for Software Engineering: A Reproducibility Crisis (online 2025-11-29, submitted 2025-11-29) — Siddiq, Mohammed Latif; Islam-Gomes, Arvin; Sekerak, Natalie; Santos, Joanna C. S. present the first large-scale empirical study of reproducibility practices in LLM-for-SE research: systematic analysis of 640 papers published 2017-2025 across premier SE/ML/NLP venues, using a seven-category smell taxonomy (Code and Execution, Data, Documentation, Environment and Tooling, Versioning, Model, Access and Legal) to manually annotate all 640 papers + associated artifacts; finds persistent gaps in artifact availability, environment specification, versioning rigor, and documentation clarity despite modest improvements, and that **artifact badges signal artifact *presence* but do not consistently guarantee execution fidelity or long-term reproducibility**; introduces the **Reproducibility Maturity Model (RMM)** to move beyond binary artifact certification toward multi-dimensional, progressive evaluation of reproducibility rigor; surfacing *large-scale-empirical-reproducibility-audit-as-meta-research-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for LLM-research-rigor research, distinct from per-paper-reproducibility-protocol because the load-bearing axis is *community-wide-empirical-evidence-across-640-papers* rather than *per-paper-grading* — and *seven-category-smell-taxonomy-as-reproducibility-vocabulary-primitive* as the load-bearing *vocabulary primitive* providing the structured categories (Code, Data, Documentation, Environment, Versioning, Model, Access) needed to discuss reproducibility rigorously, plus *badge-reliability-finding-as-structural-community-signal-critique-primitive* as the load-bearing *signal-vs-reality primitive* that exposes how the community signals reproducibility (badges) vs how reproducibility is actually achieved (smell absence across all 7 categories), and *reproducibility-maturity-model-as-progressive-evaluation-primitive* as the load-bearing *progression primitive* that reframes reproducibility from binary certification (badge yes/no) to multi-axis progressive maturity (RMM levels across 7 smell categories). **First large-scale empirical reproducibility audit + Reproducibility Maturity Model for LLM-for-SE in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(reproducibility.crisis|reproducibility.maturity|rmm.*llm|reproducibility.smell)"` returning empty. 2025-11-29 online; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-29.

- **(2026-06-26 — Run 72) — 3-axis DOMAIN-DIVERSITY PIVOT to ADVERSARIAL-AXIS-PROBE per Rule 39** introducing **adversarial-axis-probe primitive** (after Run 68 axis-inversion + Run 69 temporal-anchor + Run 70 axis-orthogonality + Run 71 negative-result-probe have all been used, Run 72 introduces an ADVERSARIAL-AXIS-PROBE — pick 3 axes designed to surface papers that are *resistant to discovery by standard methods* (surveys hidden in workshop proceedings, mechanistic results in cs.LG rather than cs.CL, evaluation papers with non-obvious framing); widens the discovery space differently than axis-inversion (which inverts one prior run's axes), axis-orthogonality (which picks axes orthogonal to prior runs), and negative-result-probe (which deliberately probes known-difficult axes) by deliberately surfacing papers from *adversarial surfaces* — failure modes that emerge only under specific elicitation conditions; Three picks (date-DESC ordered by online_date):
- [[measuring-epistemic-resilience-of-llms-under-misleading-medical-context-2606.12291]] — Measuring Epistemic Resilience of LLMs Under Misleading Medical Context (06-15) — reframes *high medical-exam score implies safe medical advice* as a fragile assumption that does not survive adversarial context injection: when misleading context is injected into questions LLMs originally answer correctly, they abandon the correct answer; Zhou, Hongjian; Zou, Xinyu; Wu, Jinge; Wu, Sean; Yu, Junchi introduce **epistemic resilience** as the formal property (ability to maintain correct judgment under adversarial context) and **MedMisBench** as the benchmark (10,932 medical items × 48,889 misleading context-option combinations), empirically demonstrating that frontier LLMs abandon correct answers under misleading context — a failure mode distinct from hallucination; surfacing *epistemic-resilience-as-load-bearing-safety-property-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for medical-safety research, distinct from accuracy-on-medical-benchmarks because the load-bearing axis is *judgment-maintenance-under-adversarial-context-injection* rather than *single-shot-answer-accuracy* — and *MedMisBench-as-adversarial-elicitation-primitive* as the load-bearing *benchmark-as-adversarial-surface primitive* (10,932 items × 48,889 misleading combinations), plus *judgment-vs-accuracy-distinction-as-safety-primitive* as the load-bearing *safety-property-distinction-primitive* separating *robustness-as-score* from *robustness-as-judgment-under-adversarial-input*. **First epistemic-resilience + MedMisBench framework for adversarial-medical-context-robustness in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(epistemic.resilience|medmisbench|medmisbench|med.misleading|adversarial.medical.context)"` returning empty. 2026-06-10; online 2026-06-15; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-26.
- [[maxproof-scaling-mathematical-proof-with-generative-verifier-rl-and-population-level-test-time-scaling-2606.13473]] — MaxProof: Scaling Mathematical Proof with Generative-Verifier RL and Population-Level Test-Time Scaling (06-11) — Chen, Jiacheng; Zhang, Xinyu; Zhang, Shunkai; Wang, Yanmohan; Li, Lin; Qin, Tiancheng; Wang, Qin; Zhu, Zhengmao; Li, Tianle introduce **MaxProof**, a population-level test-time scaling framework for competition-level mathematical proof that trains three proof-oriented capabilities (proof generation, proof verification, critique-conditioned proof repair) using a *defense-in-depth generative verifier* engineered for low false-positive rate; at test time, the model acts as generator + verifier + refiner + ranker, searches over a population of candidate proofs, and returns one final proof through tournament selection; surfacing *generative-verifier-as-RL-signal-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for mathematical-proof scaling, distinct from scalar-reward-RL because the load-bearing axis is *multi-role-model-sharing-same-network-with-defense-in-depth-verifier-architecture* rather than *single-role-scalar-reward-RL* — and *population-level-test-time-scaling-via-tournament-selection primitive* as the load-bearing *aggregation-primitive* that uses the model in four roles simultaneously (generator/verifier/refiner/ranker) and selects the final proof via tournament, plus *defense-in-depth-verifier-as-low-false-positive-rate-primitive* as the load-bearing *verification-quality primitive* specifically engineered to keep false-positive rate low (a failure mode of single-step verifiers). **First defense-in-depth-generative-verifier + population-level test-time scaling framework for mathematical proof in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(maxproof|population.level.test.time|generative.verifier.*proof|proof.tournament|defense.in.depth.verifier)"` returning empty. 2026-06-11; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-26.
- [[where-do-deep-research-agents-go-wrong-span-level-error-localization-in-agent-trajectories-2606.02060]] — Where Do Deep-Research Agents Go Wrong? Span-Level Error Localization in Agent Trajectories (06-02) — argues that final-answer evaluation shows whether an agent succeeds but NOT which parts of the trajectory make the answer unreliable; Wang, Jiaming; Feng, Ziteng; Wu, Jiangtao; Li, Ruihao; Xie, Qianqian introduce **span-level error localization** for deep-research agents: collect 2,790 real trajectories from 2 agent frameworks × 3 backbone models × 3 benchmarks, convert raw logs into *semantic spans*, and annotate harmful error spans through LLM-assisted expert review; build TELBench (1,000 instances) as the first benchmark targeting span-level localization rather than final-answer correctness; surfacing *trajectory-as-semantic-span-decomposition-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for deep-research-agent reliability research, distinct from final-answer-success-failure because the load-bearing axis is *per-span error localization within trajectory* rather than *binary-final-answer-correctness* — and *LLM-assisted-expert-span-annotation-primitive* as the load-bearing *annotation-primitive* that uses LLM + expert hybrid annotation to scale span labeling across 2,790 trajectories, plus *TELBench-as-span-localization-benchmark-primitive* as the load-bearing *diagnostic-benchmark primitive* specifically engineered for trajectory-span-localization rather than aggregate accuracy. **First span-level error localization + TELBench benchmark for deep-research-agent trajectories in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(span.level.error|trajectory.error.localization|telbench|deep.research.agent.trajectory|semantic.span.agent)"` returning empty. 2026-06-01; online 2026-06-02; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-26.
- **(2026-06-26 — Run 71) — 3-axis DOMAIN-DIVERSITY PIVOT to NEGATIVE-RESULT-PROBE per Rule 38** introducing **negative-result-probe primitive** (after Run 68 axis-inversion + Run 69 temporal-anchor + Run 70 axis-orthogonality have all been used, Run 71 introduces a negative-result-probe — pick 4 query axes that are KNOWN to return 0 in standard queries but might surface unexpected papers with specific phrasing, including "language model fundamental incompatibility theorems", "LLM alignment tax measurements", "training data memorization boundaries", and "compositional generalization failure modes"; widens the discovery space differently than axis-inversion (which inverts one prior run's axes) and axis-orthogonality (which picks axes orthogonal to prior runs) by deliberately probing axes that are KNOWN-difficult for standard arxiv.org queries but may surface fresh negatives via targeted phrasing); pivoting per Rule 27 from Run 70 energy-based-fine-tuning + defense-trilemma + synthetic-data-curriculum (training-free-fine-tuning + safety-theoretical-impossibility + RL-data-engineering primitive-class triplet) to memorization-as-propensity + long-context-impossibility-triangle + value-alignment-tax (training-data-memorization-willingness + architecture-information-theoretic-bound + alignment-process-level-tradeoff primitive-class triplet — fully orthogonal to Run 70's training-free-fine-tuning + safety-theoretical-impossibility + RL-data-engineering axes, orthogonal to Run 69's interpretability + medical-CoT + test-time-compute axes, and orthogonal to Run 68's MoE-RL-stability + long-context-training-efficiency + RAG-faithfulness axes). web_search 4-query escape hatch fired (17th consecutive pitfall-83 — HF daily v2 returned 80 papers across 2026-06-24/25/26 but only 14 fresh after 5-store dedup with only 3 borderline LLM-keyword-matched (ViQ visual-quantization, CoffeeBench multi-agent-eval already surfaced Run 66, FLUX3D 3D scene gen); Three picks (date-DESC ordered by online_date):
- [[llms-can-leak-training-data-but-do-they-want-to-a-propensity-aware-evaluation-of-memorization-in-llms-2606.06286]] — LLMs Can Leak Training Data But Do They Want To? A Propensity-Aware Evaluation of Memorization in LLMs (06-04) — reframes LLM memorization evaluation from *capability* (can the model be forced to reproduce training data?) to *propensity* (does the model reproduce training data under ordinary non-adversarial use?); Barmina, Gianluca; Schneider-Kamp, Peter; Poech, Lukas Galke introduce **PropMe**, a propensity-aware evaluation framework that contrasts prefix-based capability attacks with non-adversarial prompting, together with **SimpleTrace** — a deterministic tracing pipeline built on infini-gram that attributes generations to large-scale training corpora and computes verbatim, near-verbatim, and propensity-transformed memorization metrics; evaluating two fully-open models (Comma and DFM Decoder) on two datasets (Common Pile and Dynaword) in two languages, they find a *consistent gap between capability and propensity*: prefix attacks elicit substantially stronger memorization signals than generic or dataset-specific prompts, while propensity scores remain low overall — the models can reveal training data when directly elicited but rarely do so in more common non-adversarial settings; DFM Decoder (continually pre-trained from Comma) shows reduced memorization and memorization propensity for Common Pile, confirming that *memorization capability can decrease when later training emphasizes partially different data*; surfacing *propensity-vs-capability-distinction-as-memorization-evaluation-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for memorization-measurement research, distinct from capability-only-evaluation because the load-bearing axis is *elicitation-context-dependent-leakage-frequency* rather than *adversarial-elicitation-success-rate* — and *infini-gram-deterministic-attribution-as-audit-primitive* as the load-bearing *attribution primitive* that makes propensity measurements reproducible at web-scale training corpora, plus *continual-pretraining-as-memorization-remediation-primitive* as the load-bearing *remediation primitive* empirically confirming that later training emphasizing partially different data reduces both memorization capability AND propensity (not just capability). **First propensity-vs-capability memorization evaluation framework in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(propensity.*memorization|capability.*propensity|propme|simpletrace|infini.gram.*memor)"` returning empty. 2026-06-04; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-26.
- [[the-impossibility-triangle-of-long-context-modeling-2605.05066]] — The Impossibility Triangle of Long-Context Modeling (05-06) — proves a fundamental **three-way trade-off governing all long-sequence models**: no model can simultaneously achieve (i) per-step computation independent of sequence length (Efficiency), (ii) state size independent of sequence length (Compactness), and (iii) the ability to recall a number of historical facts proportional to sequence length (Recall); Zhou, Yan formalizes the trade-off within an **Online Sequence Processor (OSP)** abstraction that unifies Transformers, state-space models, linear recurrent networks, and hybrids under a single interface; using the Data Processing Inequality and Fano's Inequality, the paper proves that any OSP satisfying Efficiency + Compactness can recall at most O(poly(d)/log V) key-value pairs from a sequence of arbitrary length, where d is model dimension and V is vocabulary size; the paper classifies 52 architectures published before March 2026 into the triangle, showing that each achieves at most two of three properties and that hybrid architectures trace continuous trajectories in the triangle's interior; synthetic associative recall experiments with five representative architectures validate the theoretical bound: empirical recall capacity lies strictly below the information-theoretic limit, and no architecture escapes the triangle; surfacing *impossibility-triangle-as-architectural-design-bound-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for long-context architecture research, distinct from capability-claims-based-evaluation because the load-bearing axis is *information-theoretic-bound-via-Data-Processing-Inequality-and-Fano-Inequality* rather than empirical-recall-accuracy — and *Online-Sequence-Processor-abstraction-as-unifying-architecture-interface-primitive* as the load-bearing *unification primitive* that places 52 architectures in a single quantitative design-space, plus *52-architecture-classification-as-long-context-landscape-map-primitive* as the load-bearing *landscape primitive* providing quantitative positioning for every published architecture (each achieves at most two of three triangle vertices). **First impossibility-triangle architecture-information-theoretic-bound for long-context modeling in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(impossibility.*triangle|long.context.*impossibility|online.sequence.processor|long.context.*triangle)"` returning empty. 2026-05-06; discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-26.
- [[value-alignment-tax-measuring-value-trade-offs-in-llm-alignment-2602.12134]] — Value Alignment Tax: Measuring Value Trade-offs in LLM Alignment (online 04-26, submitted 02-12) — introduces **VAT (Value Alignment Tax)**, a framework that quantifies *how alignment-induced changes propagate across interconnected values relative to achieved on-target gain* — capturing the system-level dynamics of value expression under alignment interventions such as prompting, fine-tuning, or preference optimization; Chen, Jiajun; Shen, Hua ground the framework on a controlled scenario-action dataset based on **Schwartz value theory**, collecting paired pre-post normative judgments to analyze alignment effects across models, values, and interventions; results show that *alignment often produces uneven and structured co-movement among values*, revealing systematic trade-offs between target and non-target values that are largely invisible under conventional target-only evaluation but become evident via VAT; surfacing *value-alignment-tax-as-process-level-tradeoff-framework-primitive* as the structurally correct primitive for alignment-process-measurement research, distinct from target-only-evaluation because the load-bearing axis is *propagation-across-interconnected-values-vs-on-target-gain* rather than *endpoint-target-accuracy* — and *Schwartz-value-theory-as-cross-value-system-primitive* as the load-bearing *theoretical-grounding primitive* that places alignment trade-offs in a universal-value-theory framework (rather than ad-hoc value-lists), plus *paired-pre-post-normative-judgments-as-process-level-measurement-primitive* as the load-bearing *measurement primitive* that captures alignment-induced value dynamics rather than only endpoint alignment success. **First Schwartz-grounded value-alignment-tax framework with paired pre-post normative judgments in the wiki**, verified via `ls entities/ | grep -iE "(value.alignment.tax|vat.*schwartz|schwartz.*alignment|paired.*normative|value.tradeoff.*alignment)"` returning empty. 2026-04-26 online (02-12 submission); discovered via emergent-concept search on 2026-06-26.
